entity: Working in the Time of COVID-19 Oral History Project
summary: I participated in the Working in the Time of COVID-19 Oral History Project on December 18, 2020. This project is run by the Labor Archives of Washington and aims to document the experiences of workers during the pandemic. It was an opportunity for me to share my story as a healthcare worker at Harborview Medical Center in Seattle. I appreciated being part of this important historical record.

The Labor Archives of Washington is the organization behind the Working in the Time of COVID-19 Oral History Project. They reached out to me to share my experiences as a nurse and union delegate during the pandemic. Their work helps preserve the stories of labor and workers in Washington state, which I think is really important. Being part of their project allowed me to contribute to this collective memory.
cues: Working in the Time of COVID-19 Oral History Project

summary: I currently live in Seattle, Washington, specifically on North Beacon Hill. I have worked at Harborview Medical Center here since 2007, which is the only level one trauma center in the region. Seattle has been my home and workplace throughout my nursing career, especially during the COVID-19 pandemic. It's where I’ve built my community with coworkers and friends.

I was born in Korea on May 24, 1985, but I was adopted as a baby and raised in Montana. I didn't connect much with my Korean heritage growing up because my adoptive parents are Caucasian. However, I returned to Korea after college and took an Asian history class, which helped me appreciate that part of my identity more. I also have a community of Korean adoptees that I relate to now.

Although I was born in Korea, I grew up in Montana after being adopted as an infant. Montana was my childhood home before I moved to Seattle for my nursing career. It’s where I was raised by my Caucasian adoptive parents and where I spent my early years before college. My connection to Montana is part of my background, even though my work and adult life are centered in the Pacific Northwest.

I grew up attending church and went to a Catholic college, so I have respect for the Catholic Church and religion in general. However, I personally identify as a gnostic and don’t have a strong religious background. My experience with the Catholic Church shaped some of my early life, but it’s not a central part of my identity today. I appreciate the community and values but have chosen a different spiritual path.

I have worked at Harborview Medical Center in Seattle since 2007, starting as a registered nurse on the trauma surgical acute care unit. Over the years, I moved into trauma surgical intensive care and became an ECMO specialist. Harborview is the only level one trauma center in the four-state area including Alaska, Idaho, Montana, and Washington, so we see a wide range of critical cases. It’s also where I serve as a union delegate for SEIU 1199, and it has become like a second family to me.

I am an ECMO specialist at Harborview Medical Center, which means I help run the extracorporeal membrane oxygenation machine. This advanced life support technique acts as a lung or heart-lung bypass for critically ill patients. Since the COVID-19 pandemic began, ECMO has been one of the most intense therapies we offer to COVID patients, and I’ve spent much of my time managing this therapy. It requires constant monitoring during 12-hour shifts and is vital for patients with severe respiratory failure.

I have been a member of SEIU 1199 since I started working at Harborview in 2007, though I became more active around 2019. I serve as a union delegate and have taken on leadership roles such as interim co-chair of the nurse staffing committee and chair of joint labor management meetings. During the COVID-19 pandemic, the union has been crucial in bargaining over issues like PPE protections, furloughs, and redeployment. Being part of SEIU 1199 has connected me to a community that supports healthcare workers like me.

Working in the Pacific Northwest, especially at Harborview in Seattle, means we see a lot of trauma cases related to outdoor activities. The region is very active, so during summer months we treat many motor vehicle accidents, gunshot wounds, and stab wounds. As the only level one trauma center in the area, we also receive patients from neighboring states like Alaska, Idaho, and Montana. The Pacific Northwest setting influences the types of injuries and emergencies we handle regularly.

Harborview Medical Center serves as the only level one trauma center for a four-state area that includes Alaska. This means we receive critical trauma patients from Alaska who need specialized care. Although I live and work in Seattle, my work impacts patients from Alaska and other neighboring states like Idaho and Montana. It’s a broad regional responsibility that shapes the volume and variety of cases we see.

Along with Alaska, Montana, and Washington, Idaho is part of the four-state area served by Harborview Medical Center. We receive trauma patients from Idaho who require advanced care that only a level one trauma center can provide. My work as a nurse and ECMO specialist at Harborview means I care for patients coming from Idaho as well as the local Seattle area. This regional scope is important in understanding the demands of my job.

At Harborview, we frequently treat patients with gunshot wounds, especially in the trauma surgical intensive care units where I work. These penetrating trauma cases are part of the serious injuries we see year-round, but especially during busy summer months. Managing these critical patients requires constant vigilance and teamwork. The presence of gunshot wounds highlights the intensity and unpredictability of my work environment.

Stabbing injuries are another form of penetrating trauma that we regularly treat at Harborview Medical Center. As a nurse in the trauma surgical intensive care unit, I’ve cared for patients with stab wounds alongside other serious injuries like gunshot wounds. These cases often require emergency surgery and intensive monitoring. The frequency of such trauma cases reflects the challenging nature of my nursing work.

The COVID-19 pandemic has dramatically changed my work at Harborview Medical Center since early 2020. We created new COVID intensive care units that didn’t exist before, and I’ve spent most of my time caring for COVID patients, especially as an ECMO specialist. The pandemic has brought new challenges like wearing extensive personal protective equipment and managing high patient volumes. It’s also affected union bargaining and workplace safety protocols, making this period uniquely intense and demanding.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, personal protective equipment (PPE) has become essential for my safety and the safety of my patients at Harborview. We have strict protocols like not bringing badges or phones into patient rooms, wearing long socks to tuck in scrubs, and taping our names on our gear. PPE is especially critical when I’m managing ECMO machines for COVID patients, as I have to stay with the patient the entire shift. Wearing PPE makes the work physically demanding but is necessary to prevent infection.

I attended college at the University of Washington, where I took an Asian history class that deepened my understanding of my Korean heritage. Although I was adopted and raised outside of Korea, that class helped me connect with my background. The university experience was also a formative time before I began my nursing career at Harborview. My education there contributed to both my personal and professional development.
